text: The rate and composition of sweat loss can vary considerably within and among individual athletes, necessitating personalized sweat testing rather than generic hydration guidelines.
The simplest and most accurate method to assess whole-body sweating rate is via changes in body mass during exercise.
Non-sweat sources of mass change and trapped sweat in clothing are confounding factors that can reduce accuracy of body-mass-based sweating rate measurement.
Sweat sodium concentration varies depending on collection method (whole body vs. localized), timing/duration of collection, skin cleaning procedure, sample storage/handling, and analytical technique.

Factors affecting intra- and interindividual variability in sweating rate and sweat sodium concentration include exercise intensity, environmental conditions, heat acclimation, aerobic capacity, body size/composition, protective equipment, sex, maturation, aging, diet, and hydration status.
Heat acclimation is a modifiable factor that impacts both sweating rate and sweat sodium concentration, and should be accounted for when interpreting sweat test results across training phases.

Personalized fluid and electrolyte replacement strategies should be guided by individual sweat testing data collected under standardized conditions representative of the athlete's actual training or competition environment.
